// src/data/locations.js

export const LOCATIONS = {
  nz: {
    id: 'nz',
    name: 'Aotearoa',
    subtitle: 'New Zealand',
    language: 'Te Reo Māori',
    philosophy: 'Kaitiakitanga',
    philosophyMeaning: 'Guardianship of the natural world — you belong to the land, the land does not belong to you.',
    landscape: 'Native bush, fern-lined river valleys, the call of birds at dusk.',
    palette: {
      bg: '#0d1f15',
      glow1: '#1a3d22',

    },
    animal: {
      id: 'kereru',
      name: 'Kererū',
      englishName: 'Native Wood Pigeon',
      description: 'Bold, clumsy, and deeply loved. The kererū is the only bird large enough to swallow native fruits whole, making it essential to forest regeneration.',
      trust: ['A flutter in the canopy above your camp.', 'They land briefly on a branch nearby, then leave.', 'They eat fallen fruit near your garden.', 'They sit close enough to see the iridescent green on their wings.', 'They come every morning now.'],
      namingPrompt: "They've landed on your windowsill and looked directly at you. What will you call them?",
      gifts: ['a shiny pebble', 'a small feather', 'a seed you don\'t recognise'],
      emoji: '🕊️'
    },
    plants: [{
      id: 'kumara',
      name: 'Kūmara',
      english: 'Sweet potato',
      emoji: '🍠',
      cost: 8,
      harvestDays: 3,
      culturalNote: 'Brought to Aotearoa by Polynesian navigators. Planting kūmara was a sacred act — karakia (prayer) was said over the soil before seeds were placed.',
      expandedNote: 'Māori developed sophisticated kūmara storage pits (rua kūmara) to preserve harvests through winter. The kūmara was so valued it was offered to atua (gods) at harvest time.'
    }, {
      id: 'kawakawa',
      name: 'Kawakawa',
      english: 'Pepper tree',
      emoji: '🌿',
      cost: 6,
      harvestDays: 2,
      culturalNote: 'A sacred plant in Te Ao Māori. The holey leaves — eaten by caterpillars — are considered the most potent, worn in wreaths at tangihanga (funerals).',
      expandedNote: 'Kawakawa is used in rongoā Māori (traditional medicine) to treat pain, skin conditions, and digestive issues. It grows where people gather — it follows human presence.'
    }, {
      id: 'harakeke',
      name: 'Harakeke',
      english: 'Flax',
      emoji: '🌾',
      cost: 10,
      harvestDays: 4,
      culturalNote: 'The harakeke plant is a metaphor for whānau (family). The central shoot is the child, surrounded by parents, surrounded by grandparents. You never cut the heart.',
      expandedNote: 'Weavers could identify a master weaver\'s work by their harakeke — each family tended their own variety over generations. The fibre was used for everything from baskets to fishing nets to clothing.'
    }, {
      id: 'puha',
      name: 'Pūhā',
      english: 'Sowthistle',
      emoji: '🥬',
      cost: 5,
      harvestDays: 1,
      culturalNote: 'A wild green gathered from roadsides and riverbanks. Boiled with pork bones, pūhā is one of the most beloved traditional Māori dishes — simple, nourishing, free.',
      expandedNote: 'Considered a weed by settlers, pūhā was a critical food source for Māori through times of scarcity. Knowledge of where it grew was passed through families.'
    }, {
      id: 'feijoa',
      name: 'Feijoa',
      english: 'Feijoa',
      emoji: '🍈',
      cost: 15,
      harvestDays: 6,
      culturalNote: 'Now deeply embedded in New Zealand life — the scent of autumn. Originally from South America, brought by settlers, now beloved by everyone who grew up here.',
      expandedNote: 'Not native, but so woven into New Zealand identity that it belongs here now. Every neighbourhood has a feijoa tree and a neighbour who leaves bags of them at the gate.'
    }],

      culturalNote: 'True wasabi grows only in cold, clear mountain streams. It has been cultivated in Japan for over 1,000 years and was used medicinally before it was used as food.',
      expandedNote: 'Most wasabi outside Japan is horseradish paste with green dye. Real wasabi loses its flavour within 15 minutes of being grated — it must be eaten immediately. A sharkskin grater is traditional.'
    }, {
      id: 'shiso',
      name: 'Shiso',
      english: 'Perilla leaf',
      emoji: '🍃',
      cost: 6,
      harvestDays: 2,
      culturalNote: 'Used in Japan for over 2,000 years. Red shiso gives umeboshi (pickled plum) its colour. Green shiso is eaten with sashimi — its antimicrobial properties make it a natural food-safe garnish.',
      expandedNote: 'In Edo-period Japan, shiso was believed to have saved lives during a famine. A young person eating a red shellfish was saved by a doctor who prescribed shiso — giving it the name "紫蘇" meaning "purple revitalisation."'
    }, {
      id: 'bamboo',
      name: 'Takenoko',
      english: 'Bamboo shoot',
      emoji: '🎋',
      cost: 8,
      harvestDays: 3,
      culturalNote: 'Bamboo shoots emerge in spring and must be harvested young — they become woody within days. In Japanese culture, bamboo represents resilience: it bends in the storm but does not break.',
      expandedNote: 'Satoyama communities relied on bamboo groves for construction, food, and tools. Each community maintained their grove collectively — harvesting was timed and shared.'
    }, {
      id: 'yuzu',
      name: 'Yuzu',
      english: 'Yuzu citrus',
      emoji: '🍋',
      cost: 15,
      harvestDays: 6,
      culturalNote: 'Yuzu is never eaten directly — only its zest and juice are used. On the winter solstice (Tōji), Japanese people take yuzu baths to ward off colds and bring good fortune.',
      expandedNote: 'It takes a yuzu tree up to 18 years to fruit from seed. Grafted trees fruit sooner, but gardeners say the seed-grown tree has more character. Patience is built into the plant itself.'
    }],

      expandedNote: 'When settlers arrived and fenced camas prairies for cattle grazing, it was an act of cultural destruction as much as land theft. Loss of camas access was loss of food sovereignty.'

      expandedNote: 'Salal leaves are still used by florists worldwide — exported from the Pacific Northwest in huge quantities. The plant itself is almost indestructible, thriving in deep shade.'

      expandedNote: 'Coast Salish weavers developed cedar bark weaving techniques that are among the most sophisticated textile traditions in the world. The knowledge nearly disappeared through residential schools — its revival is ongoing.'

      namingPrompt: "A fawn has walked right up to you in the morning mist and pressed their nose to your hand. What will you call them?",

      name: 'Fraoch',
      english: 'Heather',
      emoji: '🌸',
      cost: 6,
      harvestDays: 3,
      culturalNote: 'White heather is considered lucky — rare enough that finding it was a gift. Heather ale is one of the oldest fermented drinks in the world, brewed in Scotland for over 4,000 years.',
      expandedNote: 'Heather was used to stuff mattresses, thatch roofs, and make rope. Beekeepers sought moorland near heather because heather honey has a unique gelatinous texture unlike any other. Nothing was wasted.'
    }, {
      id: 'rowan',
      name: 'Caorann',
      english: 'Rowan tree',
      emoji: '🍒',
      cost: 12,
      harvestDays: 5,
      culturalNote: 'Planted at the doorway of every Highland home to ward off ill intent. Rowan berries make a sharp, bright jelly eaten with game. The tree is considered sacred — cutting one down invites misfortune.',
      expandedNote: 'In Gaelic cosmology, the rowan connects the worlds. Its red berries were threaded as protective charms. In some traditions, a rowan planted by your grandmother\'s hand was the most powerful protection of all.'
    }, {
      id: 'bog_myrtle',
      name: 'Roid',
      english: 'Bog myrtle',
      emoji: '🌿',
      cost: 7,
      harvestDays: 3,
      culturalNote: 'Rubbed on skin as a natural midge repellent — essential in the Scottish Highlands. Used to flavour ale before hops became common. It grows only in wet, peaty ground.',
      expandedNote: 'The scent of bog myrtle crushed in your hand is the scent of the Highlands. Highlanders used it in sachets in linen chests — its scent lasts for years. It was used medicinally for fever and pain.'
    }, {
      id: 'sea_kale',
      name: 'Cal Mara',
      english: 'Sea kale',
      emoji: '🥦',
      cost: 8,
      harvestDays: 3,
      culturalNote: 'A coastal plant, eaten since prehistoric times. The young shoots are blanched in spring — tender, slightly nutty. Coastal communities harvested it alongside seaweed as their main spring vegetables.',
      expandedNote: 'Crofting communities (small-scale Highland farmers) had sophisticated knowledge of coastal foraging that supplemented their small plots. This knowledge was disrupted by the Highland Clearances in the 18th and 19th centuries.'
    }],
    structures: [{
      id: 'tent',
      name: 'Bothag',
      english: 'Stone shelter',
      cost: 0,
      stage: 1,
      emoji: '⛺'
    }, {
      id: 'hut',

      emoji: '🛖'
    }, {
      id: 'garden_bed',

      emoji: '🪴'
    }, {
      id: 'fire_pit',
      name: 'Teine',
      english: 'Peat fire',
      cost: 30,
      stage: 2,
      emoji: '🔥'
    }, {
      id: 'stone_wall',
      name: 'Gàrradh',
      english: 'Dry stone wall',
      cost: 40,
      stage: 2,
      emoji: '🪨'
    }, {
      id: 'porch',
      name: 'Sgàil',
      english: 'Sheltered overhang',
      cost: 80,
      stage: 3,
      emoji: '🏠'
    }],
    animalItems: [{
      id: 'hay',
      name: 'Bundle of hay',
      cost: 5,
      happinessGain: 10,
      emoji: '🌾'
    }, {
      id: 'salt_lick',
      name: 'Salt lick',
      cost: 12,
      happinessGain: 20,
      emoji: '🧂'
    }, {
      id: 'shelter',
      name: 'Stone shelter nook',
      cost: 35,
      happinessGain: 40,
      emoji: '🪨'
    }, {
      id: 'apples',
      name: 'Windfall apples',
      cost: 8,
      happinessGain: 15,
      emoji: '🍎'
    }],
    culturalEvents: [{
      month: 4,
      name: 'Bealltainn',
      description: 'Beltane. Fires lit on hilltops across the Highlands to welcome summer. Cattle were driven between two fires for protection. Tonight your fire burns a little brighter.'
    }]
  },
  okinawa: {
    id: 'okinawa',
    name: 'Uchinaā',
    subtitle: 'Okinawa',
    language: 'Uchinaguchi',
    philosophy: 'Nuchi du Takara',
    philosophyMeaning: '"Life is the treasure." Everything else is secondary. Live gently, live long, live with others.',
    landscape: 'Coral coast, hibiscus hedges, the sound of eisa drums at evening.',
    palette: {
      bg: '#081518',
      glow1: '#0d2530',
      glow2: '#091d28',
      accent: '#5cc8c8',
      soft: '#8de0e0'
    },
    animal: {
      id: 'ryukyu_rabbit',
      name: 'Amamī Usagi',
      englishName: 'Ryukyu Rabbit',
      description: 'One of the most ancient rabbit species on earth. Dark, small, nocturnal. Found only on two islands. They have survived for millions of years by being very quiet and very careful.',
      trust: ['A rustling in the undergrowth after dark.', 'Something has been nibbling your garden at night.', 'A small dark shape moves at the edge of your lantern light.', 'They sit completely still and let you look at them for a moment.', 'They come close now. Very slowly. On their own terms.'],
      namingPrompt: "In the lantern light, they've sat still long enough for you to really see them — ancient eyes, dark fur. What will you call them?",
      gifts: ['a small excavated hollow near your garden', 'a tuft of dark fur on a branch', 'they\'ve cleared a patch of weeds near your plants'],
      emoji: '🐇'
    },
    plants: [{
      id: 'goya',
      name: 'Gōyā',
      english: 'Bitter melon',
      emoji: '🥒',
      cost: 7,
      harvestDays: 3,
      culturalNote: 'The centrepiece of Okinawan cuisine. Bitter, cooling, eaten stir-fried with tofu and egg in gōyā champurū. Okinawans say the bitterness builds resilience — you learn to love what is difficult.',
      expandedNote: 'Okinawa has one of the highest concentrations of centenarians in the world. Researchers partially attribute this to gōyā — it has compounds that may support blood sugar regulation and longevity. The elders eat it daily.'
    }, {
      id: 'beni_imo',
      name: 'Beni-imo',
      english: 'Purple sweet potato',
      emoji: '🟣',
      cost: 8,
      harvestDays: 3,
      culturalNote: 'A vivid purple sweet potato grown only in Okinawa\'s red soil. It sustained the population through famines and wars. Everything purple in Okinawan cuisine — tarts, ice cream, chips — comes from beni-imo.',
      expandedNote: 'Introduced from the Americas via China in the 17th century, beni-imo adapted to Okinawan soil and became more distinctly itself over centuries. It belongs here now. The colour is extraordinary.'
    }, {
      id: 'hibiscus',
      name: 'Bussōge',
      english: 'Hibiscus',
      emoji: '🌺',
      cost: 6,
      harvestDays: 2,
      culturalNote: 'Drunk as a red tea, used in offerings, worn in hair at festivals. In Ryukyuan culture, hibiscus marks entrances — planted at gateways to welcome visitors and honour ancestors.',
      expandedNote: 'The Ryukyu Kingdom had sophisticated diplomatic relationships with China, Japan, and Southeast Asia for centuries. Their trading culture was built on respect and gift-giving — hibiscus tea was always served to guests.'
    }, {
      id: 'shikuwasa',
      name: 'Shikuwāsā',
      english: 'Flat lemon',
      emoji: '🍋',
      cost: 10,
      harvestDays: 4,
      culturalNote: 'A tiny, intensely sour citrus native to Okinawa and Taiwan. Eaten green as a condiment, or left to ripen orange and eaten fresh. It grows along old field boundaries — planted as living fences.',
      expandedNote: 'Shikuwasa contains high levels of nobiletin, a compound being studied for its potential role in Okinawan longevity. The elders squeeze it on everything. It tastes like sunshine and electricity.'
    }],
    structures: [{
      id: 'tent',
      name: 'Yashiki',
      english: 'Simple shelter',
      cost: 0,
      stage: 1,
      emoji: '⛺'
    }, {
      id: 'hut',
      name: 'Ryukyuan home',
      english: 'Red-tiled house',
      cost: 50,
      stage: 2,
      emoji: '🛖'
    }, {
      id: 'garden_bed',
      name: 'Hatake',
      english: 'Vegetable garden',
      cost: 20,
      stage: 1,
      emoji: '🪴'
    }, {
      id: 'fire_pit',
      name: 'Kamado',
      english: 'Clay cooking stove',
      cost: 30,
      stage: 2,
      emoji: '🔥'
    }, {
      id: 'stone_wall',
      name: 'Hiija',
      english: 'Coral stone wall',
      cost: 40,
      stage: 2,
      emoji: '🪸'
    }, {
      id: 'porch',
      name: 'Nūja',
      english: 'Shaded veranda',
      cost: 80,
      stage: 3,
      emoji: '🏠'
    }],
    animalItems: [{
      id: 'greens',
      name: 'Wild greens',
      cost: 5,
      happinessGain: 10,
      emoji: '🌿'
    }, {
      id: 'shelter',
      name: 'Grass burrow',
      cost: 20,
      happinessGain: 25,
      emoji: '🪹'
    }, {
      id: 'water',
      name: 'Stone water dish',
      cost: 10,
      happinessGain: 15,
      emoji: '💧'
    }, {
      id: 'leaves',
      name: 'Dry leaf pile',
      cost: 8,
      happinessGain: 12,
      emoji: '🍂'
    }],
    culturalEvents: [{
      month: 7,
      name: 'Okinawan Bon',
      description: 'Eisa drumming fills the evening air as communities dance to guide ancestor spirits home for the summer visit. The rhythm is joyful, not mournful. Your camp feels less lonely tonight.'
    }]
  }
};
export const LOCATION_LIST = Object.values(LOCATIONS);
_c = LOCATION_LIST;
var _c;
$RefreshReg$(_c, "LOCATION_LIST");

sourcesContent:
// src/data/locations.js

export const LOCATIONS = {
  nz: {
    id: 'nz',
    name: 'Aotearoa',
    subtitle: 'New Zealand',
    language: 'Te Reo Māori',
    philosophy: 'Kaitiakitanga',
    philosophyMeaning: 'Guardianship of the natural world — you belong to the land, the land does not belong to you.',
    landscape: 'Native bush, fern-lined river valleys, the call of birds at dusk.',
    palette: { bg: '#0d1f15', glow1: '#1a3d22', glow2: '#0f2a18', accent: '#6dbf8a', soft: '#a8d5b5' },
    animal: {
      id: 'kereru',
      name: 'Kererū',
      englishName: 'Native Wood Pigeon',
      description: 'Bold, clumsy, and deeply loved. The kererū is the only bird large enough to swallow native fruits whole, making it essential to forest regeneration.',
      trust: ['A flutter in the canopy above your camp.', 'They land briefly on a branch nearby, then leave.', 'They eat fallen fruit near your garden.', 'They sit close enough to see the iridescent green on their wings.', 'They come every morning now.'],
      namingPrompt: "They've landed on your windowsill and looked directly at you. What will you call them?",
      gifts: ['a shiny pebble', 'a small feather', 'a seed you don\'t recognise'],
      emoji: '🕊️',
    },
    plants: [
      { id: 'kumara', name: 'Kūmara', english: 'Sweet potato', emoji: '🍠', cost: 8, harvestDays: 3, culturalNote: 'Brought to Aotearoa by Polynesian navigators. Planting kūmara was a sacred act — karakia (prayer) was said over the soil before seeds were placed.', expandedNote: 'Māori developed sophisticated kūmara storage pits (rua kūmara) to preserve harvests through winter. The kūmara was so valued it was offered to atua (gods) at harvest time.' },
      { id: 'kawakawa', name: 'Kawakawa', english: 'Pepper tree', emoji: '🌿', cost: 6, harvestDays: 2, culturalNote: 'A sacred plant in Te Ao Māori. The holey leaves — eaten by caterpillars — are considered the most potent, worn in wreaths at tangihanga (funerals).', expandedNote: 'Kawakawa is used in rongoā Māori (traditional medicine) to treat pain, skin conditions, and digestive issues. It grows where people gather — it follows human presence.' },
      { id: 'harakeke', name: 'Harakeke', english: 'Flax', emoji: '🌾', cost: 10, harvestDays: 4, culturalNote: 'The harakeke plant is a metaphor for whānau (family). The central shoot is the child, surrounded by parents, surrounded by grandparents. You never cut the heart.', expandedNote: 'Weavers could identify a master weaver\'s work by their harakeke — each family tended their own variety over generations. The fibre was used for everything from baskets to fishing nets to clothing.' },
      { id: 'puha', name: 'Pūhā', english: 'Sowthistle', emoji: '🥬', cost: 5, harvestDays: 1, culturalNote: 'A wild green gathered from roadsides and riverbanks. Boiled with pork bones, pūhā is one of the most beloved traditional Māori dishes — simple, nourishing, free.', expandedNote: 'Considered a weed by settlers, pūhā was a critical food source for Māori through times of scarcity. Knowledge of where it grew was passed through families.' },
      { id: 'feijoa', name: 'Feijoa', english: 'Feijoa', emoji: '🍈', cost: 15, harvestDays: 6, culturalNote: 'Now deeply embedded in New Zealand life — the scent of autumn. Originally from South America, brought by settlers, now beloved by everyone who grew up here.', expandedNote: 'Not native, but so woven into New Zealand identity that it belongs here now. Every neighbourhood has a feijoa tree and a neighbour who leaves bags of them at the gate.' },
    ],
    structures: [
      { id: 'tent', name: 'Whare Puni', english: 'Sleeping hut', cost: 0, stage: 1, emoji: '⛺' },
      { id: 'hut', name: 'Whare', english: 'Timber hut', cost: 50, stage: 2, emoji: '🛖' },
      { id: 'garden_bed', name: 'Māra Kai', english: 'Food garden', cost: 20, stage: 1, emoji: '🪴' },
      { id: 'fire_pit', name: 'Ahi', english: 'Sacred fire', cost: 30, stage: 2, emoji: '🔥' },
      { id: 'storage', name: 'Rua', english: 'Underground store', cost: 40, stage: 2, emoji: '🪨' },
      { id: 'porch', name: 'Tūāhu', english: 'Sheltered porch', cost: 80, stage: 3, emoji: '🏠' },
    ],
    animalItems: [
      { id: 'berries', name: 'Tāwhara berries', cost: 5, happinessGain: 10, emoji: '🫐' },
      { id: 'perch', name: 'Rātā branch perch', cost: 20, happinessGain: 25, emoji: '🌳' },
      { id: 'water', name: 'Fresh water bowl', cost: 10, happinessGain: 15, emoji: '💧' },
      { id: 'nest', name: 'Woven nest box', cost: 35, happinessGain: 40, emoji: '🪹' },
    ],
    culturalEvents: [
      { month: 6, name: 'Matariki', description: 'The rising of the Pleiades marks the Māori New Year. A time to remember those who have passed, to celebrate the present, and to set intentions for the coming year. Your camp is quiet tonight. Look up.' },
    ],
  },

  japan: {
    id: 'japan',
    name: 'Satoyama',
    subtitle: 'Japanese Countryside',
    language: 'Japanese',
    philosophy: 'Satoyama',
    philosophyMeaning: 'The space between mountain and village — land tended as commons, neither wild nor owned.',
    landscape: 'Cedar forests, terraced rice paddies, mountain mist at dawn.',
    palette: { bg: '#1a1218', glow1: '#2d1f2a', glow2: '#1f1520', accent: '#e8a4b8', soft: '#f0c8d5' },
    animal: {
      id: 'tanuki',
      name: 'Tanuki',
      englishName: 'Raccoon Dog',
      description: 'In Japanese folklore, the tanuki is a shapeshifter — mischievous, lucky, and fond of sake. They leave small gifts and occasionally rearrange your things.',
      trust: ['Something moved at the edge of your camp at night.', 'You find a smooth stone you don\'t remember placing.', 'A round shape watches you from the treeline.', 'They waddle into camp, completely unbothered by you.', 'They sleep near your fire sometimes now.'],
      namingPrompt: "They've knocked over your water bowl and are looking at you with no remorse. What will you call them?",
      gifts: ['a coin (5 bonus coins)', 'a small clay figure', 'an acorn'],
      emoji: '🦝',
    },
    plants: [
      { id: 'wasabi', name: 'Wasabi', english: 'Wasabi', emoji: '🌱', cost: 12, harvestDays: 5, culturalNote: 'True wasabi grows only in cold, clear mountain streams. It has been cultivated in Japan for over 1,000 years and was used medicinally before it was used as food.', expandedNote: 'Most wasabi outside Japan is horseradish paste with green dye. Real wasabi loses its flavour within 15 minutes of being grated — it must be eaten immediately. A sharkskin grater is traditional.' },
      { id: 'shiso', name: 'Shiso', english: 'Perilla leaf', emoji: '🍃', cost: 6, harvestDays: 2, culturalNote: 'Used in Japan for over 2,000 years. Red shiso gives umeboshi (pickled plum) its colour. Green shiso is eaten with sashimi — its antimicrobial properties make it a natural food-safe garnish.', expandedNote: 'In Edo-period Japan, shiso was believed to have saved lives during a famine. A young person eating a red shellfish was saved by a doctor who prescribed shiso — giving it the name "紫蘇" meaning "purple revitalisation."' },
      { id: 'bamboo', name: 'Takenoko', english: 'Bamboo shoot', emoji: '🎋', cost: 8, harvestDays: 3, culturalNote: 'Bamboo shoots emerge in spring and must be harvested young — they become woody within days. In Japanese culture, bamboo represents resilience: it bends in the storm but does not break.', expandedNote: 'Satoyama communities relied on bamboo groves for construction, food, and tools. Each community maintained their grove collectively — harvesting was timed and shared.' },
      { id: 'yuzu', name: 'Yuzu', english: 'Yuzu citrus', emoji: '🍋', cost: 15, harvestDays: 6, culturalNote: 'Yuzu is never eaten directly — only its zest and juice are used. On the winter solstice (Tōji), Japanese people take yuzu baths to ward off colds and bring good fortune.', expandedNote: 'It takes a yuzu tree up to 18 years to fruit from seed. Grafted trees fruit sooner, but gardeners say the seed-grown tree has more character. Patience is built into the plant itself.' },
    ],
    structures: [
      { id: 'tent', name: 'テント', english: 'Camp shelter', cost: 0, stage: 1, emoji: '⛺' },
      { id: 'hut', name: 'Kominka', english: 'Old farmhouse', cost: 50, stage: 2, emoji: '🛖' },
      { id: 'garden_bed', name: 'Hatake', english: 'Kitchen garden', cost: 20, stage: 1, emoji: '🪴' },
      { id: 'fire_pit', name: 'Irori', english: 'Sunken hearth', cost: 30, stage: 2, emoji: '🔥' },
      { id: 'torii', name: 'Torii', english: 'Garden gate', cost: 45, stage: 2, emoji: '⛩️' },
      { id: 'porch', name: 'Engawa', english: 'Wooden veranda', cost: 80, stage: 3, emoji: '🏠' },
    ],
    animalItems: [
      { id: 'sweet_potato', name: 'Yaki-imo', cost: 5, happinessGain: 10, emoji: '🍠' },
      { id: 'sake_bowl', name: 'Small sake bowl', cost: 15, happinessGain: 20, emoji: '🍶' },
      { id: 'den', name: 'Cosy den basket', cost: 35, happinessGain: 40, emoji: '🧺' },
      { id: 'leaves', name: 'Pile of autumn leaves', cost: 8, happinessGain: 12, emoji: '🍂' },
    ],
    culturalEvents: [
      { month: 3, name: 'Hanami', description: 'Cherry blossom season. Families and friends gather under blooming trees, eat, drink, and sit with impermanence. Your camp has a visitor today — someone who just wanted to sit quietly nearby.' },
    ],
  },

  thailand: {
    id: 'thailand',
    name: 'Pa Dong',
    subtitle: 'Northern Thailand',
    language: 'Kham Mueang',
    philosophy: 'Kin Khao Rue Yang',
    philosophyMeaning: '"Have you eaten rice yet?" — the traditional greeting. Food, generosity, and community are inseparable.',
    landscape: 'Jungle clearing, bamboo groves, the smell of rain on hot earth.',
    palette: { bg: '#1a1508', glow1: '#2d2210', glow2: '#1f1a08', accent: '#d4a017', soft: '#e8c84a' },
    animal: {
      id: 'elephant',
      name: 'Chang Noi',
      englishName: 'Elephant Calf',
      description: 'In northern Thailand, elephants were partners in forest work for centuries — never truly domesticated, always respected as beings with their own will and memory.',
      trust: ['A sound like a river from deep in the trees.', 'Broken branches at the edge of your clearing.', 'A young elephant watches you eat from a distance.', 'They come close enough to take fruit from your hand.', 'They return every day now, always around dusk.'],
      namingPrompt: "They've taken a mango from your hand very gently and are looking at you with ancient eyes. What will you call them?",
      gifts: ['a smooth river stone', 'a bunch of wild bananas', 'a tuft of grass woven into a circle'],
      emoji: '🐘',
    },
    plants: [
      { id: 'lemongrass', name: 'Takhrai', english: 'Lemongrass', emoji: '🌿', cost: 5, harvestDays: 2, culturalNote: 'Used in every northern Thai kitchen. Lemongrass grows wild along riverbanks and is harvested year-round. The base is used in cooking; the leaves are dried for herbal medicine.', expandedNote: 'Karen hill tribe healers use lemongrass in steam treatments for fever and headaches. The scent is believed to clarify the mind. It is planted near doorways to welcome good spirits.' },
      { id: 'galangal', name: 'Kha', english: 'Galangal', emoji: '🫚', cost: 7, harvestDays: 3, culturalNote: 'Often confused with ginger but sharper, more piney. Kha is essential to tom kha soup — one of the oldest recorded Thai dishes, traced to the Lanna Kingdom.', expandedNote: 'The Lanna people (northern Thailand\'s pre-Thai civilisation) developed a distinct cuisine from the lowland Thai. Kha was traded along ancient spice routes through the mountains into Yunnan, China.' },
      { id: 'butterfly_pea', name: 'Anchan', english: 'Butterfly pea flower', emoji: '💙', cost: 6, harvestDays: 2, culturalNote: 'The flowers brew a vivid blue tea that turns purple when lime juice is added. Used in ceremonial offerings and as a natural food dye for sticky rice and desserts.', expandedNote: 'In traditional medicine, anchan is used for hair growth and memory. It is planted along the edges of spirit houses — its colour is considered auspicious, connecting the earthly and spirit worlds.' },
      { id: 'sticky_rice', name: 'Khao Niao', english: 'Sticky rice', emoji: '🍚', cost: 10, harvestDays: 4, culturalNote: 'In the north, sticky rice is life. Eaten by hand, shaped into small balls and dipped into sauces. Sharing your sticky rice container with someone is an act of intimacy.', expandedNote: 'Northern Thai communities plant rice according to the lunar calendar. The first planting is preceded by ceremony — offerings to Mae Phosop, the rice goddess, asking permission to begin.' },
    ],
    structures: [
      { id: 'tent', name: 'Phataen', english: 'Bamboo shelter', cost: 0, stage: 1, emoji: '⛺' },
      { id: 'hut', name: 'Ruan', english: 'Wooden house on stilts', cost: 50, stage: 2, emoji: '🛖' },
      { id: 'garden_bed', name: 'Suan Khruea', english: 'Kitchen garden', cost: 20, stage: 1, emoji: '🪴' },
      { id: 'fire_pit', name: 'Fai', english: 'Cooking fire', cost: 30, stage: 2, emoji: '🔥' },
      { id: 'spirit_house', name: 'San Phra Phum', english: 'Spirit house', cost: 40, stage: 2, emoji: '🏯' },
      { id: 'porch', name: 'Chan', english: 'Open-air platform', cost: 80, stage: 3, emoji: '🏠' },
    ],
    animalItems: [
      { id: 'mango', name: 'Fresh mango', cost: 5, happinessGain: 10, emoji: '🥭' },
      { id: 'banana', name: 'Bunch of bananas', cost: 8, happinessGain: 15, emoji: '🍌' },
      { id: 'mud_bath', name: 'Mud wallow', cost: 40, happinessGain: 50, emoji: '💦' },
      { id: 'shade', name: 'Bamboo shade structure', cost: 30, happinessGain: 35, emoji: '🎋' },
    ],
    culturalEvents: [
      { month: 11, name: 'Yi Peng', description: 'Thousands of paper lanterns rise into the night sky over Chiang Mai. Each one carries a wish, a worry, something to release. Your fire is bright tonight.' },
    ],
  },

  pnw: {
    id: 'pnw',
    name: 'Xʷməθkʷəy̓əm',
    subtitle: 'Pacific Northwest',
    language: 'Lushootseed',
    philosophy: 'Reciprocity',
    philosophyMeaning: 'You take only what you need. You give back what you can. The forest remembers.',
    landscape: 'Old-growth cedar, rain-dark ferns, the smell of earth after rain.',
    palette: { bg: '#0d1510', glow1: '#152515', glow2: '#0f1d12', accent: '#7ab87a', soft: '#a8d4a8' },
    animal: {
      id: 'bear',
      name: 'Black Bear Cub',
      englishName: 'Black Bear',
      description: 'The bear is never truly yours. They visit. They leave. They return on their own terms. In Coast Salish tradition, the bear is a teacher of self-sufficiency and the art of preparation.',
      trust: ['Scratch marks on a cedar near your camp.', 'A pile of overturned stones near your garden.', 'A bear cub watches you from the treeline for a long time, then disappears.', 'They eat berries near your camp, ignoring you completely.', 'They sleep at the edge of your clearing sometimes. On their terms.'],
      namingPrompt: "They've paused at the edge of your camp and looked back at you before disappearing into the trees. What will you call them?",
      gifts: ['a handful of huckleberries', 'a pine cone', 'nothing — but their footprints were close to your door this morning'],
      emoji: '🐻',
    },
    plants: [
      { id: 'camas', name: 'Sqʷəlíx̌', english: 'Camas bulb', emoji: '🌸', cost: 8, harvestDays: 4, culturalNote: 'Camas prairies were carefully tended by Coast Salish peoples for thousands of years — the bulbs are a staple food, sweet when slow-cooked. The prairies were maintained through controlled burning.', expandedNote: 'When settlers arrived and fenced camas prairies for cattle grazing, it was an act of cultural destruction as much as land theft. Loss of camas access was loss of food sovereignty.' },
      { id: 'salal', name: 'Sháx̌əl', english: 'Salal berry', emoji: '🫐', cost: 6, harvestDays: 2, culturalNote: 'Dried and pressed into cakes for winter storage. Salal berries were one of the most important preserved foods on the coast — trading them was a form of alliance-building between communities.', expandedNote: 'Salal leaves are still used by florists worldwide — exported from the Pacific Northwest in huge quantities. The plant itself is almost indestructible, thriving in deep shade.' },
      { id: 'cedar', name: 'Xpey\'', english: 'Western Red Cedar', emoji: '🌲', cost: 20, harvestDays: 7, culturalNote: '"The tree of life." Cedar bark was used for clothing, baskets, rope, and shelter. The tree was never fully harvested — strips of bark were taken carefully, allowing the tree to heal.', expandedNote: 'Coast Salish weavers developed cedar bark weaving techniques that are among the most sophisticated textile traditions in the world. The knowledge nearly disappeared through residential schools — its revival is ongoing.' },
      { id: 'fiddlehead', name: 'Fiddlehead fern', english: 'Fiddlehead', emoji: '🌀', cost: 5, harvestDays: 2, culturalNote: 'Harvested only in the first days of spring when the fronds are still tightly coiled. A short season — if you miss it, you wait another year. Eaten steamed or sautéed.', expandedNote: 'The timing of fiddlehead emergence was used as a calendar marker. When fiddleheads appeared, it was time to move to spring fishing grounds. Ecological knowledge as timekeeping.' },
    ],
    structures: [
      { id: 'tent', name: 'Camp shelter', english: 'Lean-to', cost: 0, stage: 1, emoji: '⛺' },
